recommendations.title: Vectorize `verify_proof()` batch processing
recommendations.recommendation: Batch proof verification already demonstrates 160.8% throughput improvement when vectorized. Ensure this code path is on by default, as it dramatically reduces CPU-bound per-item overhead. For batch=100, throughput scaled from 95.7 → 249.7 ops/s. Memory also decreased by 0.036 MB. This is the single highest ROI fix in the dataset.
recommendations.action: Merge FIX-2 branch; enforce vectorized path in verify_proof() for all batch sizes ≥ 2. Add regression test pinning P99 < 5 ms at batch=100.

recommendations.title: JIT-compile & pool aggregate() at high node counts
recommendations.recommendation: JIT compilation for the aggregation kernel at n_nodes=100 yields a 38.9% mean latency reduction and 63.6% throughput gain. The marginal memory cost (+0.14 MB) is well justified. Apply the same JIT strategy at lower node counts (≥25) to recapture latency spikes seen at nodes=25 (P99 12.1 ms vs median 5.4 ms — jitter ratio 2.2x).
recommendations.action: Merge FIX-1. Lower JIT activation threshold from nodes=100 to nodes=25. Profile memory allocation patterns to avoid excessive GC pressure.

recommendations.title: Eliminate P99 tail-latency spikes (GC / allocation storms)
recommendations.recommendation: The large delta between median and P99 latencies across aggregate() and verify_proof() indicates periodic allocation bursts. Strategies: (1) pre-allocate result buffers and reuse them across calls, (2) switch from per-call heap allocation to a memory pool, (3) reduce intermediate Python object creation in hot loops, (4) profile with tracemalloc to identify the heaviest allocations. Target: P99/median ≤ 1.5x for all ops.
recommendations.action: Add memory pool for aggregate() internal buffers. Profile verify_proof() with py-spy to locate GC-trigger hotspots. Set alerting threshold: P99 > 3× median = regression.

recommendations.title: Validate & enforce O(d log n) aggregation complexity
recommendations.recommendation: SDK claims O(d log n) complexity. Empirical data shows latency grows roughly linearly with node count (×25.7x for ×100 nodes), suggesting either log-n behavior is not fully achieved or constant-factor overheads dominate at this scale. At 100 nodes, throughput drops to 44.9 ops/s — insufficient for real-time FL at scale. Investigate: is the aggregation truly tree-structured, or falling back to sequential?
recommendations.action: Instrument aggregate() to log actual tree depth. Verify tree-reduce is activated. Benchmark at nodes=[200, 500, 1000] to confirm log-n trend. Target: 100 ops/s @ n=100.

recommendations.title: Revert / investigate FL round E2E regression (FIX-4)
recommendations.recommendation: FIX-4 significantly degraded FL round end-to-end performance, adding 35.6% mean latency and 26.3% throughput loss. Baseline fl_round_e2e(10 nodes) already shows high P95 (15.95 ms) and P99 (16.77 ms) — almost 1.6× the median (10.16 ms). FIX-4 makes this worse. Possible causes: synchronization overhead, unnecessary sequentialization of parallel steps, or lock contention introduced by the fix. Block this from merging.
recommendations.action: BLOCK FIX-4 merge. Root-cause the regression using line profiling. Decompose fl_round_e2e into sub-spans (node init, aggregation, attestation) to identify the introduced bottleneck.

recommendations.title: Investigate WASM load latency — suspicious size-invariance
recommendations.recommendation: Load times should scale with WASM payload size. The flat profile strongly suggests load_wasm() is NOT actually reading/parsing the payload — it may be returning a cached or mock result. This is a correctness risk: production deployments with large WASM models will have unexpectedly higher latency than benchmarks indicate. Verify the function actually parses bytes, check for memoization that bypasses I/O.
recommendations.action: Audit load_wasm() implementation for caching/mocking. Add a byte-level checksum validation. Benchmark with cache-cold runs (unique file paths per iteration).

recommendations.title: Deprioritize FIX-3 — negligible attest() improvement
recommendations.recommendation: attest() is already highly optimized at 4,811 ops/s and ~0.21 ms mean latency. Engineering effort on FIX-3 yields near-zero user-visible improvement. Redirect attention to REC-01 through REC-05 for maximum impact. Maintain current attest() implementation; add a performance regression guard to prevent future degradation.
recommendations.action: Close FIX-3 as 'no meaningful gain'. Add CI performance guard: attest() mean must remain < 0.3 ms and throughput > 4,000 ops/s.

recommendations.title: Cap memory growth in high-node aggregation workloads
recommendations.recommendation: While 0.46 MB peak is small in absolute terms, linear memory growth will compound at n=500+ nodes. Implement incremental aggregation so only active-window node states are held in memory. Use streaming aggregation where possible (e.g., Welford's algorithm for running statistics) to achieve O(1) memory instead of O(n).
recommendations.action: Implement streaming aggregation for large node counts. Add memory high-water mark reporting to benchmark output. Target: < 0.1 MB overhead per 100-node batch.

recommendations.title: Implement automated performance regression CI gates
recommendations.recommendation:
Enforce benchmark-based CI gates on every PR for the 5 critical operations. Suggested thresholds (derived from current baselines):
  • verify_proof(batch=100): P99 < 6 ms, throughput > 140 ops/s
  • aggregate(nodes=100): mean < 25 ms, throughput > 40 ops/s
  • fl_round_e2e(10 nodes): mean < 12 ms, P99 < 18 ms
  • attest(): mean < 0.3 ms, throughput > 4,000 ops/s
  • load_wasm(4096KB): mean < 0.35 ms
recommendations.action: Integrate pytest-benchmark or hyperfine into CI. Fail PR if any threshold is breached. Store benchmark history in a time-series DB for trend analysis.
